claim: The minor loss of ductility and toughness arose from faster saturation of slip band refinement (slip band spacing reaching ~50 nm) and earlier occurrence of microbands due to finer grain size.
evidence_text: the minor loss of ductility and toughness was arising from the faster saturation of the slip band refinement (slip band spacing reached ~50 nm) and an earlier occurrence of microbands due to the finer grain size.

claim: Hot-forged billets of 110 mm thickness were reheated at 1150 °C and hot-rolled to 60 mm, 35 mm, and 20 mm plates (reductions 45%, 68%, 82%), denoted HR45, HR68, HR82, with 4, 6, and 9 passes respectively, then water quenched.
evidence_text: The hot-forged billets were initially reheated at 1150 °C and then hot-rolled into plates with thicknesses of 60 mm, 35 mm, and 20 mm (hot-rolling reduction: 45%, 68%, 82%), which were denoted as HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively, and then water quenching to room temperature. The number of passes during hot rolling were 4, 6, and 9 for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples.

claim: The density of the steel was 6.82 g/cm^3, representing ~13% density reduction compared to pure iron.
evidence_text: The density of the investigated steel is 6.82 g/cm^3 (measured using Archimedes’ principle). In comparison to pure iron, a density reduction of ~13% was attained.

claim: All hot-rolled and water-quenched samples consisted of equiaxed FCC austenite grains containing a large fraction of first-order annealing twins (Σ3 boundaries).
evidence_text: After hotrolling and subsequent water quenching, the microstructures of all the samples consisted of equiaxed FCC austenite grains with a large fraction of the first-order annealing twins (〈1 1 1〉 60° 3-grain boundaries) identified inside (denoted as red lines).

claim: The length fractions of Σ3 twin boundaries relative to total high-angle boundaries (>15°) were 67% (HR45), 60% (HR68), and 52% (HR82).
evidence_text: The statistics of the Σ3 twin boundaries were measured to be 67%, 60%, and 52% (determined by length relative to the total length of the grain boundaries with misorientation angle >15°) for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

claim: The HR82 sample exhibited more extensive high-orientation-gradient regions in KAM maps; the mean geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) densities were 2.71×10^13 m^{-2} (HR45), 7.49×10^13 m^{-2} (HR68), and 9.87×10^13 m^{-2} (HR82).
evidence_text: Based on this method, the calculated mean GND densities were 2.71 × 10^13 m^{-2}, 7.49 × 10^13 m^{-2}, and 9.87 × 10^13 m^{-2} for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

claim: Micron-sized polygonal (Nb,Mo,W)C particles (1–25 μm) were aligned along the rolling direction; submicron spherical particles had mean diameters of 261±36 nm (HR45), 256±42 nm (HR68), 259±23 nm (HR82) and volume fractions ~0.193%, ~0.201%, ~0.198%.
evidence_text: The micron-sized polygonal ones were distributed along the rolling direction in the grain boundary and grain interior with sizes ranging from 1 to 25 μm. The submicron-sized spherical ones were distributed homogeneously in the austenite matrix. The mean particle sizes of the submicron-sized particles were 261 ± 36 nm, 256 ± 42 nm, and 259 ± 23 nm, and the volume fractions were ~ 0.193%, ~0.201%, and ~ 0.198%, respectively for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples.

claim: The micron- and submicron-sized second-phase particles were identified as (Nb,Mo,W)C MC-type complex carbides with an FCC structure and a lattice parameter of 4.36 Å.
evidence_text: the crystal structure of the precipitates was identified as the face-centered cubic (FCC) structure as shown in Fig. 3, and the lattice parameter was calculated as 4.36 Å. Thus, they were demonstrated to be the (Nb,Mo,W)C as a class of MCtype complex carbides.

claim: Nano-sized (Fe,Mn)3AlC κ-carbides with L′12 structure were present in all samples, with mean sizes of 0.81±0.26 nm (HR45), 0.78±0.17 nm (HR68), 0.83±0.23 nm (HR82) and volume fractions of 3.5±0.33%, 3.3±0.52%, 3.9±0.39%.
evidence_text: The mean sizes of the κ-carbides were 0.81 ± 0.26 nm, 0.78 ± 0.17 nm, and 0.83 ± 0.23 nm for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. The weak diffraction spots indicated the low volume fractions of the κ-carbides, which were 3.5 ± 0.33%, 3.3 ± 0.52%, and 3.9 ± 0.39% for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

claim: EBSD step size was 1 μm; mean grain sizes were determined by the line intercept method for grains with misorientation ≥15° (excluding twins), and boundaries with misorientation 60±0.5° were classified as twin boundaries.
evidence_text: The original data obtained from EBSD with a step size of 1 μm were analyzed by the HKL channel 5 software. The mean grain sizes for the grains with minimum misorientation angles of 15° (excluding annealing twin boundaries) were determined using the line intercept method from the EBSD data. The boundaries with a misorientation angle of 60 ± 0.5° were identified as twin boundaries.

claim: Fig. 1 displays EBSD band contrast maps, phase maps (FCC in blue) with high-angle boundaries (≥15°, black) and Σ3 twin boundaries (red), and KAM maps (0°–5°) for HR45, HR68, and HR82.
evidence_text: Fig. 1. EBSD (a–c) band contrast (BC) maps (d–f) phase maps overlaid by grain boundary maps and (g-i) KAM maps of the hot-rolled (a, d, g) HR45, (b, e, h) HR68, and (c, f, i) HR82 samples. The face-centered cubic (FCC) phase is shown in blue... Black and red lines denote high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) with misorientation ≥ 15° and Σ3 (60°〈111〉) grain boundaries, respectively. The KAM angle ranges from 0° to 5°.

claim: The mean sizes of κ‑carbides were 0.81±0.26 nm (HR45), 0.78±0.17 nm (HR68), and 0.83±0.23 nm (HR82); their volume fractions were 3.5±0.33%, 3.3±0.52%, and 3.9±0.39% for HR45, HR68, and HR82, respectively.
evidence_text: The mean sizes of the κ-carbides were 0.81 ± 0.26 nm, 0.78 ± 0.17 nm, and 0.83 ± 0.23 nm for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively. The weak diffraction spots indicated the low volume fractions of the κ-carbides, which were 3.5 ± 0.33 %, 3.3 ± 0.52%, and 3.9 ± 0.39 % for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

claim: The HR45 sample with 45% hot-rolling reduction exhibited an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 861±11 MPa, a yield strength (YS) of 480 MPa, and a total elongation (TE) of 60%.
evidence_text: The HR45 sample with a 45% rolling reduction exhibited an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 861 ± 11 MPa, a yield strength (YS) of 480 ± 0 MPa, and a total elongation of 60%.

claim: Increasing the hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82% raised UTS by ~110 MPa and YS by ~170 MPa, while uniform elongation (UE) decreased from 52.0±1.0% to 39.0±0.8% and total elongation (TE) decreased from 60±1% to 50±1%.
evidence_text: With the increase in rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, the improvement of UTS and YS reached ~110 MPa, and ~170 MPa, respectively, while the UE decreased from 52.0 ± 1.0% to 39.0 ± 0.8, and TE decreased from 60 ± 1% to 50 ± 1%.

claim: All hot-rolled samples showed Charpy V-notch impact energy above 100 J/m²; increasing reduction from 45% to 82% decreased the impact energy from 133±2.5 J/m² to 105±1.2 J/m², yet good toughness was maintained.
evidence_text: All the samples at different hot-rolling reductions possessed outstanding Charpy impact energy above 100 J/m². The increase in hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82% caused a minor loss of the Charpy impact energy from 133 ± 2.5 J/m² to 105 ± 1.2 J/m², suggesting good toughness was maintained.

claim: Tensile fracture surfaces of all hot-rolled samples displayed ductile dimples and microvoids; intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles did not harm toughness because strain/stress concentrations at their interfaces were partially relieved by debonding of intragranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and formation of microvoids.
evidence_text: The fracture surfaces of the investigated samples after the tensile test were examined using SEM, as exhibited in Fig. 6. The fractography of all the hot-rolled samples shows a typical ductile fracture with a combination of ductile dimples and various microvoids. In general, the (Nb, Mo,W)C particles located at the austenite grain boundaries ... will lead to the strain/stress concentration at their interfaces due to their deformation discrepancy with the austenite matrix. ... the strain/stress concentration at the interfaces of these intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles could be partially alleviated through the debonding of the intragranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles and the subsequent formation of microvoids ... Considering the abundantly observed ductile dimples on the fracture surface of the three investigated samples, the intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles virtually played no detrimental effect on the toughness.

claim: Compared with solution-treated austenitic stainless steels and Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, HR82 exhibited similar ductility (~50%) and toughness (~100 J/m²) but markedly higher yield strength; compared to age-hardened austenitic low-density steel, HR82 offered comparable YS (~650 MPa) with significantly superior ductility (50% vs 21%) and toughness (105 J/m² vs 35 J/m²).
evidence_text: In comparison to the austenitic stainless steels and the Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys at the solutiontreated state [26], the HR82 sample exhibited a similar level of ductility (~50%) and toughness (~100 J/m²) while the yield strength was markedly higher. The yield strength of the HR82 sample is comparable to the aging-hardened austenitic low-density steel (~650 MPa) [27,28] but possessed considerably better ductility (50% vs. 21%) [27] and toughness (105 J vs. 35 J) [28].

claim: Increasing hot-rolling reduction refined grain size through enhanced dynamic recrystallization (DRX) and possibly intensified particle-stimulated nucleation (PSN) from (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
evidence_text: This was mainly attributed to the enhanced dynamic recrystallization (DRX) for the sample subjected to larger rolling reduction. Additionally, the intenser particle-stimulated nucleation (PSN) might be another influencing factor considering the existence of a large number of $(\mathrm{Nb,Mo,W)C}$ particles

claim: The geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) density increased continuously with hot-rolling reduction: 2.71×10^13, 7.49×10^13, and 9.87×10^13 m⁻² for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.
evidence_text: The GND densities obtained from the KAM maps as already shown in Section 3.1 were $2.71 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$, $7.49 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$, and $9.87 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{m}^{-2}$ for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively.

claim: Grain boundary strengthening contributions using effective grain size including twin boundaries were 157.02 MPa, 215.84 MPa, and 276.78 MPa for HR45, HR68, and HR82, with Hall-Petch coefficient kHP = 733.43 MPa·μm^1/2 and friction stress σ0 = 203.10 MPa.
evidence_text: Hence, the contribution of grain boundary strengthening for HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples was calculated as 157.02 MPa, 215.84 MPa, and 276.78 MPa, respectively. ... the friction stress (σ0) of 203.10 MPa and the Hall-Petch coefficient (kHP) of 733.43 MPa μm^1/2 were obtained from the y-axis intercept and slope, respectively.

claim: Table 2 lists the calculated and experimental yield strengths: for HR45, σ_calc=483.9 MPa, σ_exp=480.0 MPa; HR68, 577.3 MPa, 577.0 MPa; HR82, 653.9 MPa, 648.0 MPa, with individual contributions from friction stress, precipitate shearing/bypassing, dislocation and grain boundary strengthening.
evidence_text: Table 2 The summarization of the strengthening contribution of different strengthening mechanisms to the yield strength and the experimentally tested results of the investigated samples. HR45: σ0 203.1, σp-κ 61.0, σp-(Nb,Mo,W)C 6.5, σds 56.3, σgb 157.0, σcalc 483.9, σexp 480.0; HR68: ... σcalc 577.3, σexp 577.0; HR82: ... σcalc 653.9, σexp 648.0.

claim: Figure 9(a) plots true flow stress at yielding σF0 (excluding precipitate and dislocation strengthening) versus inverse square root of mean effective grain size d_e^{-1/2}, yielding data points (d_e^{-1/2}=0.20 μm^{-1/2}, σF0=359.5 MPa; 0.28, 420.0; 0.36, 479.3).

The strain-hardening rate (dσ/dε) vs. true strain curves of all investigated samples exhibit a three-staged behavior: an initial drop after yielding (stage I), a gradual increase to peak values (stage II), and a continuous decrease until fracture (stage III).
evidence_text: The evolution trend of the strain-hardening rate of the three investigated samples was similar, which exhibited an initial drop after yielding (stage I) followed by a gradual increase to their peak values (stage II) and then a continuous decrease until fracture (stage III), indicating a three-staged strain-hardening behavior.

claim: Despite a high stacking fault energy (SFE) of ~73 mJ/m², the steel deforms by planar dislocation glide forming slip bands and Taylor lattices, attributed to glide plane softening from dislocations shearing κ-carbides.
evidence_text: Despite the high SFE of ~73 mJ/m^2, the evolution of the strain-hardening rate was quite different with other conventional high-SFE fcc metals ... the SFE of the present steel was calculated to be ~73 mJ/m2. ... planar glide ... interaction between dislocations and κ-carbides ... 'glide plane softening' ... once the κ-carbide was sheared by the leading dislocation, a preferential glide path for the subsequent dislocations would be generated ... formation of the planar slip bands and Taylor lattice.

claim: At engineering strain ε_e=0.1, the mean slip band spacing is 296.40 ± 50.06 nm for HR45, 155.02 ± 38.42 nm for HR68, and 68.05 ± 8.91 nm for HR82, indicating faster refinement with greater hot-rolling reduction.
evidence_text: the slip band spacing at the engineering strain of 0.1 reduced with increasing hot-rolling reduction, which was measured to be 296.40 ± 50.06 nm, 155.02 ± 38.42 nm, and 68.05 ± 8.91 nm for the HR45, HR68, and HR82 samples, respectively

claim: Slip band refinement saturates at a minimum spacing of approximately 50 nm; after reaching this limit, further plastic strain is accommodated by microband formation.
evidence_text: the slip band spacing barely changed after reducing to ~50 nm, which indicated that the slip band refinement process during stage II was saturated and could not provide additional strain-hardening effect by reducing the slip band spacing. The accommodation of further plastic strain should be sustained by the formation of other dislocation substructures.

claim: The three-staged strain-hardening behavior maintains a strain-hardening rate at a high level of approximately 2000 MPa over a wide plastic strain range, contributing to excellent ductility.
evidence_text: the strain-hardening rate being maintained at a high level of ~2000 MPa over a wide range of plastic strain, contributing to the excellent ductility.

claim: Interrupted tensile tests for TEM characterization of deformation substructures were performed at engineering strains ε_e of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4.
evidence_text: interrupted tensile tests at the engineering strain (ε_e) of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 were conducted and the corresponding deformation microstructures were characterized by TEM.
